speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really off lately—fatigue, weight gain, and my vision’s been blurry for about a week.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your symptoms. When did the blurred vision begin, and is it constant or intermittent?

speaker: patient
text: It started around last Wednesday—so about eight days ago—and it’s gotten progressively worse, especially when reading or driving.

speaker: patient
text: Yeah, that blurred vision—I noticed it first on April 19th, and it hasn’t improved.

speaker: doctor
text: Okay, thank you. Let me check your vitals and run some labs. First, your hemoglobin today is 136 g/L—within normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is 136 g/L—good, no anemia concern.

speaker: doctor
text: Heart rate is elevated at 118 bpm—slightly tachycardic. Any palpitations or shortness of breath?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms, exam, and labs, the primary diagnosis is Hypothyroidism. We’ll confirm with TSH and free T4.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Atorvastatin 20mg once daily at night to address your borderline cholesterol—we’ll monitor liver enzymes.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 93 mmHg—elevated but not yet hypertensive stage 2. Let’s keep an eye on it.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some dizziness when standing up quickly—could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly—it could reflect autonomic involvement or volume status. Let’s reassess after we review your thyroid panel and repeat vitals.

speaker: patient
text: My hands have felt colder than usual too, and I’m sleeping more—even with eight hours, I’m exhausted.

speaker: doctor
text: Just rechecked—your heart rate is now 130 bpm. That’s a notable increase from earlier; let’s rule out acute stress response or subclinical arrhythmia.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your worsening symptoms and rising glucose trends on recent fasting labs, I’m going to switch you to Insulin glargine 10 units once nightly to improve glycemic control.

speaker: patient
text: Oh—I didn’t realize my blood sugar was high. Does that relate to the blurry vision?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—hyperglycemia can cause osmotic lens swelling and transient blurring. Also, your diastolic pressure has risen to 103 mmHg since our earlier reading—let’s discuss lifestyle adjustments and consider antihypertensive follow-up.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense. I’ve been skipping meals sometimes because of nausea—I wonder if that’s affecting things.

speaker: doctor
text: Nausea and fatigue are common in untreated hypothyroidism—and may worsen with erratic eating. Let’s schedule a nutrition consult and repeat thyroid function tests in two weeks.

speaker: patient
text: Right—just like that reading from earlier, my hemoglobin is still 136 g/L, so at least that part hasn’t changed.
